posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
An interesting week, the release of the new Eichelmann, no real surprises but a very interesting preface, which articulates a growing frustration which a number of my colleagues and myself feel towards especially the younger generation of grower producers.

Also this week we visited one of the top grower producers in our sortiment and he echoed indepedently what Eichelmann wrote. More interesting is his decision to give up making different crus and make just one champagne incorporating the three champagnes he makes at the moment.

To Eichelmann. A lot of young producers are very effective at creating hype via social media. For us it is difficult, if we do not go with the producer, we might have missed a great oppurtunity which we could later regret. The problem with these hyped grower producers, is firstly the price and secondly the quantity. Microvinification sound great, in reality it is expensive so the price reflects more the work involved rather than the quality in the bottle.

As quantity is neglible, allocations are ridiculously small, meaning to share evenly amongst customer, one is reduced to selling one bottle per customer. Is there any real value in buying just one bottle? What is the optimal point to drink ? These young producers seem totally disenfranchised from the wishes of the customers.

Then the never ending fragmentation, with each new release one discovers a new champagne parcellaire nowhere to be found on the map. The top, the bottom, the east and west side all deserve in the produce’rs mind individual expression. The individual expression of the champagnes however is not worth expressing and if the four were blended together a better champagne would have been made.

A producer with 30 hectares said, it is enough for us to have just one parcellaire. Jacquesson with 200 000 bottles a year have identified four sites they consider worthy of a individual expression. Yet we have these young producers with 1 or 2 hectares releasing 4-6 parcallaire champagnes in miniscule quantities.

Eichelmann says, Microvinification shows how necessary it is for producers to have a brut non-vintage champagne in quantity. This is something the maisons have been preaching for a long time, stating it is important for the region. I would have to agree.

Young producers need to be drunk young if they have to build up a clientele, this is resulting in too short aging on the lees, lower dosage and lower or no sulphur. The champagnes are enjoyable in the first few years after release but they just don’t age. The arguement about terroir becomes negligble, if in 4 or 5 years the champagne is shot.

In 2017 Leclapart’s L’Aphrodisaique was an amalgamation of all the best plots, the best champagne he has producer in my opinion. Emanuel Brochet like Leclapart is capable of making champange at Krug level. His decision in the future to make just one champagne rather than three, might help this younger generation understand the benefits of blending.

posts.name: W_Shearer
posts.cooked:
I wish all champagne producers would go back to including more parcels in their NV, MV brut and extra brut cuvées. I’m thinking Pierre Peters in particular, but most all have marginalised their standard blends the past decade.
Yes, on top of that you have the new generation with their micro-vinifications…

As with everything else, things go through phases, trends and trials; the pendulum swings this way and that; in time this too shall pass.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

the new Eichelmann

who is this and can we read his writings? Nur auf Deutsch?

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Unfortunately the new edition is in german and will be translated into French. The last edition which is outdated in term of reviews of what is availabe but offers excellent producer profiles is available in english. He is highly respected by the producers and it was at their request that the last edition was published in english,

In terms of grower producers, he is the most comprehensive critic. Also I think he is quite balanced and not affiliated to any of the maisons like most of the champagne critics are in some way or another.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

In 2017 Leclapart’s L’Aphrodisaique was an amalgamation of all the best plots, the best champagne he has producer in my opinion. Emanuel Brochet like Leclapart is capable of making champange at Krug level. His decision in the future to make just one champagne rather than three, might help this younger generation understand the benefits of blending.

Those are also priced at or above the Krug level, so I’m glad the wine compares to Krug in quality.

Are there any growers making <$100 wines who blend like you advocate and whom you recommend?

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Leclapart’s L’Amateur from the 2019 vintage is his entry level champagne and after the disappointment of 2018 a really good introduction to the Leclapart style.

Natalie Falmet’s Val Cornet is a big champagne which really ages very well.

De Sousa. The 3A which is a combination of Chardonnay from Avize and Pinot Noir from Ay and Ambonnay. I prefer this to Egly’s Brut Grand Cru.

Bedel, she has an unique style, the Ente Ciel et Terre is chardonnay dominated with Meunier and Pinot Noir.

Perseval Farge, a traditional producer who works with a lot of reserve wines.

Aubry but I feel since the cousin has joined them, they have lost their edge.

Hure Freres don’t really blend, yet the Memoires is one of the best Vin Perpetuelles, much cheaper and more interesting than Bereche’s Reflet d’Antan.

From my perspective the best value champagne at the moment is Maillart’s Platine. 67% Pinot noir, 26% Chardonnay and 7% Meunier, a blend from the vintages 2016,2015,2014,2013… 3g/l dosage.

An interesting and up and coming producer is Tristan Hyest, he sells a lof Pinot Noir to Krug. He is rather eccentric and copies Kug in the way the make champagne. His champagnes are not a subsitute for Krug but never the less very interesting.

Les 7 Vigne is a blend of f 50% Chardonnay and 50% Pinot noir (20% what he calls Solera) 4g Dosage. The best in the sortiment. The italian market is crazy for his champanges.

posts.name: T. McKinley
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

Leclapart’s L’Amateur from the 2019 vintage is his entry level champagne and after the disappointment of 2018 a really good introduction to the Leclapart style.

Care to elaborate on what happened in 2018? I’ve got a bottle I picked up on a whim. The CT notes aren’t inspiring any confidence, but curious why this vintage was so problematic.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked: It was a really hot vintage and an early harvest, no or very little acidity. This was the year of the coteaux’s. Leclapart was not happy with the 2018 vintage, he told us, with 19 they are back on form.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
I don’t think it’s unique to champagne, but a lot of young growers all over have figured that that bottling micro cuvées is a profitable and effective marketing strategy.

They are able to charge more and the limited supply helps with the hype. Plenty of consumers seem to buy into that rarity hype very easily. Oh, a new culty producer that I could only buy 1-2 bottle of (if one can find it at all)? Yes please. Let me buy it so I can post on instagram how cool and in the know I am.

Distributors also seem to be less discerning now when signing up new producers. Not by desire, but almost by necessity. As you alluded to Donald, if one doesn’t sign up a new producer, another distributor will. There’s real fear of missing out the next big hit producer. So distributors snap up anything they can find. Even if the quality is not there—yet or ever. The hope seems to be for many that the quality eventually catches up to the hype that’s created. I almost draw some parallels to how some VCs operate. Investing in lots hoping that a few hit big and pay off.

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked:
Too much differentiation is going to cause fatigue for some of us who love this space of grower Champagne. Marguet may be a good example of differentiation…although he has been in this way for a while now. I’d love to try the various parcels but I neither have the means nor the want to to try and track it all down. So, I focus on the few sites I know, that I like, and I buy those regularly (meaning each release as I can find them). I don’t mind the differentiation, as long as there is some differentiation there to experience in the glass. Crayeres, Grand Ruelle, Bermonts, plus Shaman and now Yuman.

In the example of Marie Courtin, if I may use that one, there is differentiation, but it is made through vinification differences via her single site, not multiple sites. I’m good with that and I try and buy all the separate cuvees each year. Vilmart another…arguably across a few sites, although not bottled by defining site. Instead, using cepage blend ratios, and level of time sur lie and exposure to new wood.

I don’t disagree with the premise of Daniel’s point, but I am instead focusing on where the differentiation in my examples is one we can speak positively about, even with Marguet if one is willing to look for the positive in the way he does it.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I think a big aspect of champagne making is experience. I agree with you with Marie courtin and Marguet, although Marguet goes a bit overboard sometimes.

It is however a big difference if you have sites in Ambonnay, Bouzy or in Aube, but just not every site is worth expressing, and then the splitting of sites into north/south/east/west is crazy.

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

An interesting and up and coming producer is Tristan Hyest, he sells a lof Pinot Noir to Krug. He is rather eccentric and copies Kug in the way the make champagne. His champagnes are not a subsitute for Krug but never the less very interesting.

That sounded interesting but talk about too many different bottlings

IMG_80791170×2024 593 KB

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
This is the problem, this stupid obsession with expressing every supposed terroir/soil type.

Hyest is a talented champagne maker but he makes too many champagnes.
They get insulted when one only takes two or three champagnes from their selection, but they don’t seem to understand, when you only get six or twelve bottles the amount of effort and also space that is required to sell them and customers want 3 or 6 bottles not just one bottle. They taste a bottle like it, come back and do not understand whay they cannot buy more.

posts.name: Tim Williams
posts.cooked:
You can thank Burgundy for this! It’s been their model forever. Champagne is just catching on. (Germany too for that matter)

When the number of individual bottlings creeps past 4 or 5 I get decision fatigue

Edit to add: I love all of these regions, but the old BDX model of good, better, best bottling is so much easier.

posts.name: Mariano Chiaramonte
posts.cooked:
T_Williams:

You can thank Burgundy for this! It’s been their model forever

Agree, but in Burgundy is not a matter of choice, is a matter of necessity. In my opinion, the rhetoric about letting the wine express its terroir in Burgundy came after the fact that the producers were forced to produce that way, it would have been very difficult for them to make a profit otherwise.
My problem with the previous wine trend AKA “Parkerization”, was that many producers went big and bold, whether it made sense or not. Now there are many producers all around the globe that are miro-vinifying whether it makes sense or not just because it’s the new trend.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

This is the problem, this stupid obsession with expressing every supposed terroir/soil type.

Hyest is a talented champagne maker but he makes too many champagnes.
They get insulted when one only takes two or three champagnes from their selection, but they don’t seem to understand, when you only get six or twelve bottles the amount of effort and also space that is required to sell them and customers want 3 or 6 bottles not just one bottle. They taste a bottle like it, come back and do not understand whay they cannot buy more.

I was only on the Williams Selyem list for a few years maybe 15ish years ago, but this was what that was like. You’re allocated 0-1 each of a dozen+ single vineyard pinots, with little or no basis to know you’d prefer one over another, or to really learn which ones you like more than the others, and no opportunity to go back for more of one you liked.

I guess the customer’s way forward is just to pick a few each year and enjoy them and not worry about it, but I just never liked the experience.

posts.name: Tim Williams
posts.cooked: Dare I say that I have the same sentiment with Bedrock

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked: One positive other example I thought of this morning is the grower Gerbais in the Aube. His model, if I can simplify it, is do a bottling of each cepage (that is built on solera), plus a bottling of a blend of cepages, to include Rose. About half dozen wines, an opportunity to contrast…manageable. It’s intrigued me enough that I have some arriving today–the Chard and Pinot, plus the blended cuvee. I want to try them all again. Manageable.

posts.name: adam caldwell

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked: I opened the Grains de Celles Rose tonight. Delicious. Looking forward to finishing tomorrow. Lots of refreshing acid wrapping around red fruit and peach.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Well said as always Rodrigo.

But it’s exptremely important to remember that distributors tend to play it extremely safely. They buy what is established or what is hot at the moment (no offense intended to Kris Patten).

But if you want to build awareness of your wines as a new producer, you had better have something that captures people’s attention. Most distributors have thousands of wines in the portfolio, and bigger wineries are not looking to share the focus or spotlight with little wineries. If your wines get shown more than twice a year, you’re lucky(and well represented).

For most new producers, site is the most interesting story you have. And good producers work hard to have fruit from the best sites, as it’s the most defining and singular aspect of your production. I can see moving from multiple cuvees to a single bottling if the site based wines work better together, but the smart producer will do it after establishing them individually.

I’m obviously biased, and have little intention of reducing the number of cuvees we do. Like Jim Anderson, I view the small lots we do as record keeping of the sort done for centuries in Burgundy.

But I can honestly say that there is no middle ground here. Micro-lots are comfortably profitable and single cuvees sell to grocery stores after every mega-winery’s stacks are in place and whatever new fad (chocolate wine) has their facings. Then you call Wine Access, Gary V, WTSO, Underground Cellar, etc. to find homes for the rest.

My read on this thread isn’t to make less cuvees, it’s to focus individual cuvees on 1-3 markets so that there’s enough supply to offer customers more than 1-2 bottles apiece. And not to listen when a retailer wants the one they can’t have…

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
Sometimes i feel so young on this board .

Who are we to decide if some sites are worth making single vineyard wines from or not? Or what amount of dosage and sulfites they use. If you disagree with the winemakers choices, dont buy it. Some of us are really into these no dosage low sulfites Champagnes.

If they use it as a marketing trick and it works? Good on them, smart young business people. I rather want this direction than a Lenny Kravitz og Lady Gaga version.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I would say transparency is the key. When we ask producers to explain, why is this deemed worthy of being a single cru? Especially when it was not a single cru in the past.

The answers are not really satisfactory and it the producer cannot explain this, how does one explain this to a customer. Just because one is clay and the other sand is not enough.

I sometimes taste privately in a group where someone high up in the Dom Perignon chain also participates. He knows the Côte de Blancs especially well, one thing he said. For them they have better sites than Chetillons, and they deem them not worthy of a parcellaire champagne. He will not tell us where these sites are.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
This is circular reasoning. Yes, as purchasers of the wines we have a say in deciding whether certain bottlings are worthy or not. Maybe we just don’t like trying to support independent producers only to have them throw lots of bottlings at us that may not be worthy of differentiation when they could probably make a cuvee and increase availability while maintaining quality.

The thing about the Dom P celebrity branding is that they are not trying to tell us its a better wine because of some special selection the winemaker practiced their alchemy over. It’s just a fancy collectible. It’s a lot more honest in some ways than many houses charging premiums for any particular selections without first confirming it’s quality matches its advanced pricing.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
How will they increase availability? They have the grapes they have. If it goes into one or ten cuvees does not increase the quantity of grapes.

I know a lot of people pays more for single vineyard wines. And maybe the winemakers think they are better and price it accordingly. But i also think some are just trying to show different expressions. If we can taste the difference or not, and what the market does based on that is not really on the winemaker. If they think there is a big difference between one plot and another, i will let them decide on that.

We will only see more of this. Not only in Champagne.

Yes, we will not be able to taste or get our hands on all of them. But it creates more available expressions to pick from. If people think they are worth it or not is up to them. If there is no market for it then prices will decrease. But for me it feels a bit strange to put such a definite judgement on grower Champagne. Feels just like people disregarding natural wines because they don’t know what to look for and had a bad experience.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
Availability increases because they have less highly allocated bottles. Kind of simple. The fruit is a constant. If you make 10 bottlings from it, then they are less of each bottling. If you make one bottling, then there are larger quantities of that wine than there would be per each individual bottling.

Of course this leads to more cynical discussions about hype and scarcity driving pricing. While I would never suggest all producers are playing the manufactured scarcity game, I’d bet good many at least a few are.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
I do agree on a lot of that Cris. And of cause some are playing the game.

But in regards to the availability, then that would be true for some producers maybe. But most of them make very small quantities and allocations issues would likely be same. As one who has been hunting the white wines from Jura for a few years now, i would say that if the production is small enough (it often is) then it does not make much difference if they have two or thirty cuvees.

posts.name: Bennett Tyson
posts.cooked: Reading through this thread it doesn’t really sound like the issue is whether or not individual cuvees are worthwhile, it sounds more like all the producers are going in that direction whether the wine they make is good or not. If a producer is relatively one note and has trouble pulling out different characteristics from different sites, it would be better for consumers if they made a blend or cuvees that they can distinguish (ie blend + individual PM/PN/Chard bottlings vs chard from 5 different sites that taste the same).

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

I sometimes taste privately in a group where someone high up in the Dom Perignon chain also participates. He knows the Côte de Blancs especially well, one thing he said. For them they have better sites than Chetillons, and they deem them not worthy of a parcellaire champagne. He will not tell us where these sites are.

Chetillons is a pretty big vineyard and some parts are much better than others. Peters made it fashionable to put Chetillons on the label, but I often find many of the Chetillons wines to be very good rather than the great that I think many expect based on the vineyard name. Whether this expectation is mistaken/misguided is a whole different conversation.

DP’s best spots in my opinion are in Cramant and, for my palate, I think there are more interesting and better expressions/wines coming out of Cramant than any other Cote des Blancs village.

The question on blending is a good one. To me, one of the great examples of this is Lilbert’s vintage which hails from two vineyards in Cramant - Moyens and Buissons. Moyens is certainly nice enough, but not the best Cramant wine and not in a great location. Buissons is in a top location, is terrific on its own, and to me a top Cramant expression. But, when you blend a little of the Moyens into the Buissons, the total package is better than either on its own with the colder, slightly angry Moyens meshing beautifully with the fruiter, floral, bright, mineral Buissons.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Sorry Chris, but that is circular logic.

As Lasse said, you get the tonnage you get and the yields you get. The multiple cuvees add up to the same total bottles that the blend would. So you may not have access to every Ulysses Collin bottling you want but you have the same access as if he made one wine…

Except that if Ulysses Collin only made one wine it would be a lot less interesting to delve into his wines and many of us would buy less. So more would be available.

Also-Dom Perignon isn’t my favorite champagne by a mile (YMMV). However, it’s considerably more expensive than many grower wines that I prefer and quite a bit of that cost is the outlandish amount of money spent on marketing it as a fancy collectible. I don’t see that wine as a good example of why smaller growers should dump everything in one tank and then take out a loan to pay for branding materials for every Whole Foods in the world every November. If for no other reason than Dom and Veuve already have that niche completely filled.

No one is holding a gun to anyone’s head to buy every cuvee. And plenty of these sites may not be the next Les Mesnil. I personally still don’t see Meunier as amazing, so I pass on most of the new darling wines where that’s the focus. But I absolutely appreciate knowing that someone is a Meunier specialist, so I can make my buying decisions knowledgeably. If I don’t know a new site, I can post here and between FM3 and KJJ plus a few others I bet I will get a good enough response to make a reasonable decision. And if I get a $200 dud, I’m ok with saying so.

All of the OPs issues are valid inconveniences but, for my money, there’s no better wine region in the world right now than Champagne for great, delicious, interesting wines. And while low sulfur, short tiraged, inexperienced winemakers, unworthy sites, too expensive, unavailable wines are absolutely issues, it’s miles better, in my opinion, than it would be without the grower revolution and the introduction of individual terroirs.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
2012 Clos St. Sophie from Lassaigne remains one of the greatest champagnes that I have had. So while some previously unrecognized sites might need to be questioned, there’s little doubt the old order has room to be shaken up and re-examined.

Especially given global warming, which has definititively altered the wines in Champagne since the last time crus were defined.

If we’re being transparent- Retailers would generally prefer things don’t change. What’s established is easiest to sell, and wading through new or evolving regions (or new or evolving appreciation for a region) is inefficient (I’m not being a jerk, this is an honest assessment based on what is now 25+ years of selling wine). Retail is a hard place to be if things aren’t efficient.

I completely agree with you that more people should focus on experience, and it should be valued more. But that has to be balanced by the fact that as a production gets bigger the risk involved with it not selling goes up as well, and established producers who grow production often get fairly conservative with their choices. So it’s really important to have small producers who can produce wines that are not conservative in their elevage. That’s where experience really comes from, 30 years of paint by numbers winemaking isn’t going to make you an exceptional winemaker. I see that lesson all the time.

But champagne will settle itself out, what’s happening there is a pretty natural aspect of commerce, and there are going to be plenty of bumps along the road because at times social media and marketing allow story to trump the actual wines at both big houses and growers. Pricing makes negative experiences more painful than it used to be. But the perfect is the enemy of the good, and this thread seems like we’re complaining about what inconveniences us, having to wade through the jungle to find the gold instead of looking at how much more interesting Champagne is now and how good so many of the wines are.

I don’t worry much about losing Champagne to micro-vinification, I worry about losing it to global warming.

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

Sorry Chris, but that is circular logic.

As Lasse said, you get the tonnage you get and the yields you get. The multiple cuvees add up to the same total bottles that the blend would. So you may not have access to every Ulysses Collin bottling you want but you have the same access as if he made one wine…

Except that if Ulysses Collin only made one wine it would be a lot less interesting to delve into his wines and many of us would buy less. So more would be available.

So it’s not circular logic because there would be bottles available to more people and they’d be easier to sell (to explain) so aggregate demand would not fall (or might even rise), while quality could increase by thoughtful blending. Win win.

posts.cooked:

So if you blend what do you do with the lesser grapes that won’t make it in the blend, sell off in bulk?

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

If we’re being transparent- Retailers would generally prefer things don’t change. What’s established is easiest to sell, and wading through new or evolving regions (or new or evolving appreciation for a region) is inefficient (I’m not being a jerk, this is an honest assessment based on what is now 25+ years of selling wine). Retail is a hard place to be if things aren’t efficient.

Yes, I strongly agree with this. While the OP post is pitched as complains on behalf of consumers, it’s very much a retailer complaint. By making many cuvées producers have made it harder for retailers who have to explain the differences (and sometimes maybe there aren’t any!). But that is a different issue than what’s best for the consumer - I’m delighted that Bereche makes so many cuvées, for example. They’re quite different!

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Tom_G_l_a_s_g_o_w:

So if you blend what do you do with the lesser grapes that won’t make it in the blend, sell off in bulk?

If they are lesser grapes they shouldn’t be going into the single cru bottlings either, and presumably aren’t.

This conversation reminds me of the lesson that General Motors learned the hard way about having too many models with merely minor differences. They weren’t just harder to sell, they were objectively worse cars than they could have been with fewer models.

reply_to_user.name: Tom G l a s g o w

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
You have three pressings, this s required . The second called the taille is usually sold to the maisons. Dosage can deflect the problems.

Guiborat’s Tethys is a champage with the first and second pressing.

Ruppert-Leroy also uses the first and second pressing in all of their champagnes, they say it is a waste not to use the juice. However without dosage and sulphur we see how decisive RL champagnes are.

If you or I agree doesn’t really matter. Thats what they dedicated their life to, not to make it more convenient for some consumers. And how do people even know that blending them would make them better? Taste is such a subjective matter. Personally i prefer zero dosage Champagne with crazy high acidity most of the time.

LasseK:

What about the pleasure and joy of complaining? We as consumers have the absolute right to enjoy those little treats of life

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
Fair enough. I am not always sure as English is not my primary language, and languages certainly isn’t my strength…

And just to be clear, then i also like more “classic” Champagne. I just tend to focus on growers when i buy any myself.

posts.cooked: Well ofcause complaining is allowed. All my posts are me complaining about them complaining

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen
posts.cooked: At my last tasting my friends complained about drinking Krug when my fridge is full of zero dosage high acid bubbles

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I think @Frank_Murray_III touched on this earlier: what do you find more interesting as a consumer of grower Champagne:

(1) assorted bottles that differ mostly because of winemaker differences like pick date, dosage, oak,and cepage?

(2) assorted bottles that differ mostly because of vineyard or plot?

I think I’d tend to be more interested in (1).

Of course, it doesn’t have to be all one or all the other — I’m just asking which bottling differences tend to interest you more.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
It’s a big assumption to assume blending makes a better wine. Sometimes it can, but sometimes it just doesn’t work that way.

Most of all the wines almost always lose some amount of singularity, which isn’t always bad but homogenous is more of a “big house” goal IMO. Small wineries need to be aiming higher than good because bigger wineries almost always have more marketing muscle, so the little winery is the one that will get lost.

Also-the blend will unquestionably be harder to sell. It has nothing to stand out (which is why it’s so easy to explain), nothing differentiates it from the already 100% established wines of M&C, Roederer, Billecart, Paillard, Veuve, Krug, etc…
They own this area of the sparkling market like Mike Tyson owned the ring in his early 20s.

So the blend gets lost against them and goes into the small house arena, which can work (Charton-Taillet Cuvee St. Anne).

Also, we’re glossing over the fact that Champagne currently has a supply shortage. That probably won’t last forever and as it eases, the availability of the high end wines will increase and we will probably see more options in blends. But in the face of short vintages, farming costs stay the same and fixed winery costs also stay the same. Entry level blends don’t make much sense with high quality and low supply.

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked: Right. It is a big assumption. And if it doesn’t make better wines then they shouldn’t blend. But I think the examples given by Donald and Brad show that blending is likely to improve the final product in a good number of cases and those should blend.

I think @Frank_Murray_III touched on this earlier: what do you find more interesting as a consumer of grower Champagne:

(1) assorted bottles that differ mostly because of winemaker differences like pick date, dosage, oak,and cepage?

(2) assorted bottles that differ mostly because of vineyard or plot?

I think I’d tend to be more interested in (1).

Of course, it doesn’t have to be all one or all the other — I’m just asking which bottling differences tend to interest you more.

Number 1 by quite a margin for me

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Great question. With respect, I absolutely fall into category 2.

I want to know the information in category number 1, but only to help me understand the wines better in a category 2 way.

(Perhaps excluding use of sulfur, as I am pretty tired of paying for tired wines that shouldn’t be)

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
(2) is often also associated with a different cepage or blend of cepages in the particular site/plot.

I am really interested when a winemaker puts it out there that plot x is really good for cepage y, and creates a wine to show that off. Chartogne-Taillet has done a lot of that. Some of it is really convincing. Some is not.

posts.name: CJ Beazley
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

Great question. With respect, I absolutely fall into category 2.

I want to know the information in category number 1, but only to help me understand the wines better in a category 2 way.

(Perhaps excluding use of sulfur, as I am pretty tired of paying for tired wines that shouldn’t be)

Thanks for weighing in. I was thinking more what I could pick out in a blind setting, not so much what I might want to understand. That’s to say-if two bottles were vinified in exactly the same way but from different sites to pick up serious differences would be a high bar for me.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
That’s a logical thought.

Devil’s Advocate-let’s say that 75% of the time individual blending individual site cuvees could modestly improve the quality of the resulting wine. But in my experience, the resulting wine will also taste more like everyone else’s wines as well. You absolutely lose unique character when you combine wines to “get the best wine”. So quality is up, but the wine now tastes much more like everyone else’s wines. Is that really an improvement?

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
Thanks Marcus,

My comments did miss a lot of nuance into the topic that I hope this post helps bring out some of my more nuanced thoughts. Though realistically I doubt I’ll cover everything.

For one, I’m not advocating producers consolidate and blend all their parcels into larger cuvees. I like it when a producer I like makes various different wines, event if I am unable to realistic buy or try all of them. I want there to be differences in vineyards, varieties, blends, etc and to be able to taste them. That is a big part of what makes wine exciting to me.

I’m also not overly critical allocation quantities for small producers, nor do I think it’s fair to be. With rising land prices, burgeoning Champagne growers with limited funds also have limited access to vineyards and fruit. At times, they are fortunate to get access to a couple of hectares of fruit as it is. So even if all the fruit is blended into a single cuvee, the resulting production is going to be small—and for many popular producers, it’d still be less than the demand.

Your comments on producers focusing on which markets they go into is apt I think. If one is a producer with a small production, it’s nigh impossible to please all that want your wines. Even if one is allocating tightly, they still can’t get it to everyone that may want one. There’s simply not enough wine, even at 1-2 bottles a person, to go around. One needs to pick and chose where they want to have their wines. I look at Ulyse Collin as an example of that. Olivier has chosen to focus his wine sales to restaurants. Wanting his wines to be drunk at them with good food. It’s made his wines at retail less accessible and more expensive, but it allows people to drink his wines in a setting he thinks is best. The wine prices in restaurant wine lists continue to be reasonable (to me), so I am content with that trade off.

As far as the growing micro cuvée trend in champagne and the comparisons to Burgundy that have been drawn—Burgundy has had the benefit of its small size and centuries to understand the various parcels and microclimates, and which are worthy of being bottled as single vineyard designations. Champagne, to a large extent, hasn’t had that that extensive deep dive, for a number of reasons. Notably its larger size and the historical proclivity to blend much of production into MV cuvees.

In a very real way, many of the champagne growers now are doing the work Burgundy growers did centuries ago. Namely the process of trial and error of bottling individual parcels and cepages to better understand the vineyards they have. It’s a somewhat long process to figure out if a particular parcel or cuvee has its own individual voice that’s worth expressing. It’s not something that can be judged in a couple of vintages, and there’s no real shortcuts to it. While there are some geological and physiological markers that may hint at whether a certain vineyard, micro climate or blend is ‘unique’ and worthy of being bottled individually, realistically the only way one can find out is by vinifying and bottling it.

I don’t mind producers having many different cuvées, in so long as they are distinct, and speak of the place they come from. As Greg noted, sometimes there really aren’t any differences between the cuvées. At times, it’s massive, like Bereche. I love the differences in champagne (and all other wines for that matter) it’s part of what keeps things interesting

That said, while the resulting wines may all taste different, I don’t think that alone warrants continued individual bottling of certain cuvees. If one is picking and vinifying wines differently, the resulting wines are expected to taste differently. Whether that’s ‘unique’ or worthy of expressing is a different matter as far as I’m concerned. Just because a particular cuvee/parcel/etc tastes differently doesn’t mean it’s worth bottling it as such. But as I noted before, there’s no real shortcuts here, the only way to figure that out is by making those individual wines and seeing how they evolve.

Some of the critique that Donal alluded to is valid in that when one is only able to purchase a single bottle of a wine, it makes the assessment of these micro cuvees harder. As a function of small quantities and allocations, most are never really able to see how these micro cuvees evolve over time. A single bottle is merely snapshot in time and it makes it tougher to access the wine and how it age, for better or for worse.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked: I didn’t bring up Dom P. Lasse was comparing the special labeling they use to the idea of the many bottlings that are being discussed in the thread.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
I agree with most of what Rodrigo says here.

I don’t think anyone is arguing that always blending will lead to better wines. A lot of us would just like to see blends as an option again.

There is a lot of momentum, not only in Champagne, after houses start creating vineyard designates and other smaller selections. Once those get rolling then there is the feeling or the demand to keep making those in each vintage. In my experience not every SVD needs to be one every vintage. In still wines the knee jerk reaction is that appellation bottlings are of lesser quality which makes it difficult for wineries to pull back even if they wanted to. In Champagne I think its a bit easier for a house to sell such blends. I just wish many houses would opt to more often.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Cris_Whetstone:

A lot of us would just like to see blends as an option again.

Which producer’s wines do you think have gotten worse as a result of doing more single vineyards champagnes as opposed to blends?

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Rodrigo_B:

Some of the critique that Donal alluded to is valid in that when one is only able to purchase a single bottle of a wine, it makes the assessment of these micro cuvees harder. As a function of small quantities and allocations, most are never really able to see how these micro cuvees evolve over time. A single bottle is merely snapshot in time and it makes it tougher to access the wine and how it age, for better or for worse.

I think there are a couple of different issues here. If a consumer can only buy one bottle and wants to follow the evolution of the wine over time, I suspect the the size of the producer’s holding is the issue as much as anything else. Like the Haut Chardonnay from Brochet, for example. Make enough wine generally, and Collin’s Roises or Bouchard’s Croix d’Enfers being harder to find is less of an issue - the Maillons/Val Vilaine are plentiful. A consumer doesn’t have to own every wine - I can’t buy Burgundy grand crus by the case either.

On the other hand, where I strongly agree is that I’m not a big fan of micro-cuvees generally, where producers simply don’t get a lot of experience in making the wine year to year. When I see wines with 300 bottles made, whether in Champagne or Burgundy (or other regions), I tend to wonder how much experience a producer gets with less than a full barrel every year. But that’s not really a blending issue - Burgundy producers have been blending holdings too small to vinify separately into village (or generic 1er cru) cuvees for a long time.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked: Is anyone claiming any have? The idea is that there would increased availability from producers while also selling less more expensive bottlings that might not be worthy of premium pricing in every vintage.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
I only wrote that because the initial post kinda hinted that it was used as tool for marketing/hype of their own wines on social media. This could probably end in a long discussion so i will keep it short.

Basically, if i had to choose, then i would prefer marketing with a focus on whats in the bottle to whats on the bottle. If winemakers oversell the content in the bottle the hype would probably die out quickly.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
Larger blended cuvees are generally sold at much lower costs than SVD cuvees. Forgoing bottling a SVD wine and blending it into a larger cuvee can take a large financial toll on a producer. I’m sympathetic of that financial reality.

As an example, Alex Chartogne doesn’t bottle all of his single-vineyard parcels every years, but he also has 11.5 hectares of vines, so he’s able to make at least a few SVD wines every year. He also makes a significant amount of Saint-Anne yearly to give other fruit a home. If you only have a small amount of fruit to work with, there’s much less margin to work with.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
I’m largely in agreement with you. There’s a difference between a cuvee’s production volume and how much is able to be shared around with end consumers. I touched on this briefly in my post, but I’m certainly not advocating that a producer has a responsibility to ensure all their consumers get at least 6 bottles of a particular cuvee they want.

As an aside, I personally have little interest in wines I can only buy 1 or 2 bottles of. I like to buy at least 6 of pretty much everything so I can follow a wine’s evolution, but that’s a personal consumption decision, that’s not a critique on a producer’s production volume, number of cuvees or how they allocate their wines.

I also agree with you that micro cuvees make it much harder for producers to really get familiar a wine. I also wonder how much of those small productions go into a producer’s own library to see how it changes over time, I imagine not much. As an example, I drank a 13 Julian Haart Schubertslay kabi a short while back and in talking about it Julian, he remarked that even he doesn’t have any of that in his cellar.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked: I think everyone understands that. Its just somewhat apparent that the often hype driven market pays small houses to make high priced SVD cuvees whether the fruit quality supports them or not.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Cris_Whetstone:

Is anyone claiming any have? The idea is that there would increased availability from producers while also selling less more expensive bottlings that might not be worthy of premium pricing in every vintage.

If more single vineyard designates are made in such small numbers that consumers can only buy one and don’t decrease the quality of the other wines, then I don’t see the issue. The increase in availability of the blends wouldn’t be significant and there’s no requirement to buy the more expensive bottlings.

This is all very market driven, no? If customers weren’t happy with all of the single vineyard wines, they would sit on the shelves. For people who want more blends, the big houses do an admirable job (some better than others, of course).

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Chartogne-Taillet has overextended.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Rodrigo_B:

I’m largely in agreement with you. There’s a difference between a cuvee’s production volume and how much is able to be shared around with end consumers. I touched on this briefly in my post, but I’m certainly not advocating that a producer has a responsibility to ensure all their consumers get at least 6 bottles of a particular cuvee they want.

As an aside, I personally have little interest in wines I can only buy 1 or 2 bottles of. I like to buy at least 6 of pretty much everything so I can follow a wine’s evolution, but that’s a personal consumption decision, that’s not a critique on a producer’s production volume, number of cuvees or how they allocate their wines.

I also agree with you that micro cuvees make it much harder for producers to really get familiar a wine. I also wonder how much of those small productions go into a producer’s own library to see how it changes over time, I imagine not much. As an example, I drank a 13 Julian Haart Schubertslay kabi a short while back and in talking about it Julian, he remarked that even he doesn’t have any of that in his cellar.

Yes, I agree. Consider a producer like Bereche - I think his newer 1er Crus (the Ambonnay and the Ay in particular) are now his best wines, but given that they’re grand cru sites, the quantities are small. It’s possible, but hard, to get those in quantity, but those wines are great - I don’t want him to blend them into a tete de cuvee just because I can’t buy half a case. And I’m not sure I’d be able to get 6 of a Bereche grand cru tete de cuvee anyway (especially two years from now). But, that’s an entirely personal consideration - I completely understand people who don’t want to own wines they can’t have less than 3 or 6 of. However, as you say - that’s a producer’s decision, not ours. I’d be curious to see who’s going to tell Christophe Roumier to stop bottling the Musingy because they can’t get a 6 pack.

I’m always amazed how little wine some producers keep - some are just not that sentimental about their wine. I was at a dinner recently with a producer who said he has no 12s left in most of his cuvees. And he makes a fair amount of wine!

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
There’s a couple of things to that.

The first is the financial reality that comes with the decision to declassify or blend away fruit.

The second is whether the fruit from a parcel is unworthy of being bottled as a SVD vintage champagne, or is it that it’s just not as good as a previous vintage. There is a difference. There’s plenty of SVD champagne where I prefer one vintage to another. It’s a whole other story as to whether a grower should have simply blended it all into a larger cuvee.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
Greg_K:

I’d be curious to see who’s going to tell Christophe Roumier to stop bottling the Musingy because they can’t get a 6 pack.

Greg_K:

I’m always amazed how little wine some producers keep - some are just not that sentimental about their wine. I was at a dinner recently with a producer who said he has no 12s left in most of his cuvees. And he makes a fair amount of wine!

That’s always surprising to me. I can understand a lack of sentimentality for the wines, but as a wine lover I’d wish producers kept at least some of their own wines. Not for sentimental reasons, but solely for the ability for them to explore and see how their wines change over time. I’d think that’s a valuable resource for a producer, but who knows I guess…

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

That’s a logical thought.

Devil’s Advocate-let’s say that 75% of the time individual blending individual site cuvees could modestly improve the quality of the resulting wine. But in my experience, the resulting wine will also taste more like everyone else’s wines as well. You absolutely lose unique character when you combine wines to “get the best wine”. So quality is up, but the wine now tastes much more like everyone else’s wines. Is that really an improvement?

If quality is up then it is definitely an improvement.

But let’s talk about that matter of the unique character that could get lost because I don’t think it necessarily follows that the resulting wine would taste more like everyone else’s, especially in Champagne, or that that would be necessarily a bad thing depending on who the “everyone else” is.

As to that last point, I am almost certain, for example, that if critics said of a grower’s wine “this tastes like Krug” and they were telling the truth, that grower would have no trouble selling all the wine they want to sell.

But let’s say that’s not said. Are wines really likely to taste that much like everyone else’s? I don’t think it’s easy to make wine taste like another maker’s wine even if one wants to, especially in Champagne. Just a difference in disgorgement dates can make two otherwise identical champagnes taste different. Add to that the array of vinification and aging options and I don’t think lack of differentiation is something they should worry about.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Great post and I agree.

You have to pave the road to drive on it. Your commentary on Ulysses Collin is well timed. His shift to restaurants does the things you mentioned, but it’s also a clever way to deal with a temporary shortage of wine. Suddenly limiting it to restaurants alleviates the issues of splitting alocations nearly as deeply as you would have to if you kept retail in the mix. It doesn’t leave everyone happy by any stretch but it keeps your distributors reps from get ripped by frustrated accounts, and it’s a defendable choice.

I also don’t feel every site warrants a named cuvee, and I really don’t believe it’s a good long term choice to be hyper aggressive with pricing on a wine just because it’s an SVD.

I can sympathize with feeling overwhelmed from there being so many new cuvees but I don’t feel a need to try them all. And if I can only get one bottle, I’m not sure how that’s different from Krug Clos de Mesnil or Lafon Montrachet (in Oregon about a case comes into the state). It’s not optimal to only have a snapshot, but I don’t mind if it’s a really good wine. My biggest disappointments are the expensive but “meh” bottles that were less unpalatable at $50-75 than they are at $150-200.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Krug has traditionally been a very unique producer when it comes to style. So it’s highly unlikely that a grower blending their vineyard designates would arrive at that result.

I agree that champagne/sparkling wine is different than any other for resisting becoming homogenous. Each bottle has a singularly unique fermentation so even two bottles disgorged at the same time can be very, very different. Though typically more in a fermentation way(reductive/not reductive) and less from the vineyard. We’re seeing that up close right now.

But finesse is almost always lost or diminished in blending, and big houses blended for house style for years (not a bad thing for some enthusiasts). I’m not against blends, we make a Willamette Valley Pinot Noir every year but I love wines with precision and finesse and I’m for separating the cream so that customers can see the best we are able to do. I’d hate for the very best barrels to get lost in a blend just to make something with a broader reach.

posts.name: Tom G l a s g o w
posts.cooked:
I was thinking that for Marcus/Megan (MM) to have a bunch of different sparkling wines that are in separate vineyard bottlings might be more informative in the first few years, than blending to start. Blending certain vineyards might make sense after MM get more experience. Aren’t a lot of grower vineyards sites that formerly sold off grapes to the big houses?
The single vineyards also tend to sell for more.
posts.display_username: Tom G l a s g o w

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Tom_G_l_a_s_g_o_w:

I was thinking that for Marcus/Megan (MM) to have a bunch of different sparkling wines that are in separate vineyard bottlings might be more informative in the first few years, than blending to start. Blending certain vineyards might make sense after MM get more experience.

As long as they blend after vinification, they can always blend the cuvee that’s for sale and still keep some unblended bottles to see how they evolve and if they merit separate bottling. I think one, maybe two bottlings to start would be better for business. But then, Marcus is right here and it’s his business not mine.

posts.name: Tom G l a s g o w
posts.cooked: I’m sure he appreciates all our advice.
posts.display_username: Tom G l a s g o w

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
I’m ok with not owning or even trying every cuvee a producer I like makes. Though I sure as hell do my best to try all of their cuvees. To me, it’s less a feeling of being overwhelmed, and more akin to a young kid in a candy store: Oh, look! I want to try this! And that! And those over there as well! Realities, both financial and availability makes some of that hard or impossible, but I like that journey.

There’s certainly some frustration, and wonderment, at seeing mediocre wines drastically climb in price. I feel like tiny quantities and marketing hype helps. It is surprising to see some get to the $200+ price range. At that point there gets to be a lot of high quality options available to consumers. If there are people out there who like the wines and willing to pay those prices and find it worthwhile, it doesn’t really affect me—some of those I wouldn’t much care to purchase and drink at all, regardless of price. There’s certainly some wines where their current price has gone beyond my comfort level to purchase them, but such is life. On to searching for or focusing on others.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
All single site wines are blends. Why do I say that? Any time you combine the juice from two grapes, you have created a blend. You would probably respond: that’s stupid, even if true on some level. But somewhere between two grapes from the same vine and a million grapes from an appellation lies an opinion on where certain blends are more worthwhile than others.

An example: in 2018, Kelley Fox sold four wines produced from grapes grown in the Maresh vineyard. Just so that you know my level of interest, I bought all four. Are those four wines really distinctive enough to warrant four different bottlings? Why not one bottling? Why not 20? What is a “site”?

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
While I wish I could still easily buy six-packs and more of Collin, Bouchard, and other micro-cuvée producers, I’ve soothed myself reading this discussion with Filaine DMY (L16). And, magically, my disappointment evaporates.

Point being that there are many great growers. And Filaine DMY is a stonking great wine.
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
yep.

I’m going to read the deeper message here as “sometimes you should just trust the producer”. Knowing Kelley, she came to her conclusions based on her best ability at the time to provide consumers with a look at what she felt was special in the wines she sheperded from Maresh vineyard in 2018. I do know that I am really happy she bottled the Royal Ann separately in 2019…

As to what is a site, that’s a deep question, as sites are mostly comprised of sites, which are also comprised of sites. However we, in the industry, want to rationalize it to ourselves it’s subjective. I used to think of Whistling Ridge as a single site, but now I think of it as a combination of sub-sites that can be worthy of bottling individually, or put together to form a larger site. Each vintage is different, so I couldn’t tell you which it will be in any given year, and even when I think that I can it’s really consumers decision as to whether or not my choices are worth bothering with.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Unquestionably correct.

And this is a good place for feedback. Regardless of whether I make 2 or 20 cuvees, we get more than enough support from Berserkers to warrant me paying attention to what other posters think.

I’m deeply suspicious of keeping a few bottles separate and bottling blends with the majority. It almost always means hand bottling and doing things outside of our normal process for storage before bottling, not to mention keeping track of unlabeled bottles in a small winery setting (hey Cab Franc…). It’s too easy to introduce a significant alteration to the wine that wouldn’t have been present otherwise, usually for the worse, or losing track of which is which. Things are super hectic at bottling, brains are fried, and days are very long, little projects get fouled up very easily. It’s better in my mind to make 60-90 cases of something to see what it really looks like and determine what we have by giving it every chance to succeed. None of these will be $200 bottles, and I am not Cedric Bouchard so it will be ok, and those who want to see the individual sites can, and those who prefer a smaller selection can opt to wait another year or two.

But…the 2022 Durant Blanc de Blanc was a perfect match for the barrel it was raised in, and also is the one wine that didn’t go through malo-lactic at all. The others are a range between done (one wine) and partial ranging between .9 g/l and 3.56 g/l. The Durant is highest at 4g/l. Nothing happens in a vaccuum so I don’t recommend making any absolute decisions off of this, and different vineyards will handle malic acid differently but it’s still going to be very educational…

reply_to_user.name: Tom G l a s g o w

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

the 2022 Durant Blanc de Blanc was a perfect match for the barrel it was raised in, and also is the one wine that didn’t go through malo-lactic at all.

You know my feelings about that

posts.name: W_Shearer
posts.cooked: As I mentioned at the beginning of this thread, Pierre Peters Cuvée de Réserve has really been impoverished as Rodolphe has lifted out single vineyards from the cuvée.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
The geek in me enjoys tasting all these single parcellaire champagnes but I find very rarely do I want to retaste or buy. We see this with the customers. Freres Mignons are for me a really good example, a few years ago there was a massive jump in quality and the prices were ridiculously low, in that context the champagnes sold out very fast. Within two years we have seen an almost 100% price increase, last year customers complained, why are we paying so much when nothing has changed. They have a Cramant and a Cuis in their sortiment, what is really interesting here, Bereche buys grapes from them for their Cramant. Taste the Cramant side by side and one sees the difference what experience makes etc Last year the Cramant and Cuis moved fast, this year there is no real interest.

This is a phenomenon we are seeing, ultimately the market decides. In contrast to Freres Mignons, Pertois-Lebrun at first a stolid producer, no hype but step for step they have improved the quality, prices have risen constantly and the consumers understand this. If one compares their Chetillons to the massively hyped Elise Bougy’s Chetillon, the differences in every sense are incredible.

Pierre Gerbais, he has done a lot to improve and being the nieghbour to Cedric Bouchard is abonus, I like the champagnes and have tasted them all from this years releases, I like them but do they effect me like Bouchard, no, but the hype is moving in that direction. Next year I would say, take as much of Champ Viole and the Nr19 as we can get as well as the entry level Grains de Celles. La Loges, Bochot and the La grande Côtes are nice, but Do I really need them. Price wise we sell at the same level as Marie Courtin and they are not at that level.

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked: Leave Marie Courtin out of this please. Dominique is right there with Laurent Champs as my two absolute favorites. Let’s please keep them both safe and protected from the hype and price insanity.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
WilliamS:

As I mentioned at the beginning of this thread, Pierre Peters Cuvée de Réserve has really been impoverished as Rodolphe has lifted out single vineyards from the cuvée.

I believe Pierre Peters has been effectively making the Chetillons since the 90s. When do you think the impoverishment began?

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I don’t think this is true. With champagnes I tend to compare it to an orchestra and solists. How many players in an orchestra have got what it takes to stand up front and play the part of the soloist? One of the most interesting tasting I have participated in, was when we drunk Egly’s Blanc de Noir, Jacquesson Degorge Tardive and a Krug non vintage, different in style but all in terms of substance at a similiar level. We then tasted a vintage Krug and it was comparable to that moment when Anne-Sophie Mutter starts playing, just at a different level altogether.

I think, if one is going to do a single vineyard/parcellaire champagne, then it must really stand apart from the rest of the sortiment. In Burgundy, when you start tasting at Bourgogne rouge/blanc then work your way up, village, 1er cru then grand cru, the articulation of site gets more preciser and focussed, the depth and complexity increases. There are very few producers in the Champagne, who are achieving this.

The idea of pursuing terroir and making champagne are at odds with one another. Champagne is more about the expertise of the maker, the chef de cave than anything else. I think through blending one can make truly individual and unique champagnes. Most single parcel champagnes taste interesting when tasted close to the degorgement but very few profit from long term aging and the aspired for individuality does not hold up.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Even longer actually - have tasted back to 1982!

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Well it was Special Club until around 98?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Yes, from 1971 onwards IIRC.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
So many assumptions here… like what a Champagne should be or not be. That Champagnes should by definition be able to age else they are lesser wines and so on? Most people do not collect and store wine for extended periods. So maybe it is a good thing that they are fun and accessible early on, and less important how they peak at age 20+.

What if other people simply prefer something different?

But as you said, then the market will decide in the end.

I just love that we see more and more variation. People can still buy the big house names if they don’t think the grower Champagnes are worth it.

Also i do get that it might be a pain to navigate as a retailer. But that is another discussion.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I come from a gastronomy background. When you get your first position as chef de cuisine, you have so many ideas and one gets carried away and then with time one realises less is more. Cooking is not about creativity but refinement and I think it is the same with champagne. The novelty of über creativity quickly wears off, a waiter telling me for the hundredth time what this foam etc is just gets monotonous.

Coming from Burgundy to Champagne it was very easy to get into the terroir mentality of the Champagne, but we are reaching a point where it is like the über creativity and the endless expression of every stone is just risible.

I sort of held Brochet and Savart at about the same level, last week we taste both, Brochet is moving towards one champagne as he says the blend is the better than the three individual champagnes, for me the most exciting thing to happen in the region for some time. Savart it seems has peaked, the new Mont Chretiens does not deliver.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

The novelty of über creativity quickly wears off, a waiter telling me for the hundredth time what this foam etc is just gets monotonous.

But most of us do not work in the business and are not constantly exposed to this. I am not disregarding your experience or anything. But it is just worded as your view is a universal truth and hence there is an issue in Champagne right now. Maybe there isn’t? Maybe it is an issue/annoying for you and others in parts of the business, but remember that for most of us it is a hobby. And even as hobbyist we are certainly the outliers on this board, not the average consumer.

As i am based in Copenhagen, and got into wine maybe ten years ago, i was mostly exposed to the “natural wine” scene. Same with everyone i know who finds wine interesting. Because i am a big geek, by nature, i have tasted widely to try and understand wine. But most of my good friends have not and they certainly like a fresher, less rounded, but accessible style most of the time. They would pick and enjoy grower Champagnes with low dosage most of the time. It is what they like. They also like the story of winemakers, the approach to farming, the “uniqueness” of the cuvée. It is just a part of a generation that has a different view on what wine should be both from a taste and story perspective. What they also have, now that we are in our 30’s, is the money to create a market for wines like this.

I am not saying that every place in the world mirrors Copenhagen. Or that every young person in Copenhagen would agree with me. Far from it. I am just trying to say that there is certainly a market for this type of wine and for many this is what they like. That in return makes it economically sustainable for the winemakers to take this approach.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
The Peters’? It was designated Cuvee Speciale by the 90s at latest but didn’t say Chetillons on the label.

I don’t think this Chetillons plot was ever part of Cuvee Reserve, at least the best grapes. I don’t know if other sites now bottled separately or now blended into other wines used to be part of the solera used for the Reserve, which was started in the late 80s. My vague recollection is that there were three wines I could get in the States in the late 90s through the Theise portfolio - Reserve or maybe just NV then as base bottling, vintage, and Cuvee Speciale. Maybe @David_Bu3ker has a clearer memory on that.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
LasseK:

I am not saying that every place in the world mirrors Copenhagen

Montreal mirrors Copenhagen for that same generation.
Paris mirrors Copenhagen for that same generation.

Just thought I would add two examples
